text:
Azomethine imines represent an extensive class of aza-allyl 1,3-dipoles that can be employed as either transient in situ generated cycloaddition reagents or as stable synthetic precursors for their potential uses. Azomethine imines react with a broad range of hetero and homo dipolarophiles including activated and deactivated alkenes, alkynes, and allenoates, predominantly within a [3+2] context. While their employment in classical pentannulation reactions of azomethine imines is fairly well explored, their involvement in higher-order 1,3-dipolar cycloadditions in contexts of [3+3], [4+3], [3+2+2], and [5+3] reactions is not rare.

Catalytic variant of these very old reactions were investigated with the aid of heat, microwave, metallic or organocatalysts. This review focuses specifically on the enantioselective 1,3-dipolar cycloadditions that prove to be a versatile method towards the synthesis of the biologically relevant six-membered heterocyclic six-membered ring dinitrogen analogues. Although acyclic azomethine imines derived from hydrazones can afford pyrazolidines and/or pyrazolines following activation through protonation/prototropic activation as well as through acidic activation, C–N bond incorporated cyclic dipoles like isoquinolinium imides give pyrazolo[1,5-a]isoquinolines, pyrazolopyridines, and indazoloisoquinolines in their racemic as well as in enantioenriched forms. Stable N-alkylidene-3-oxo-pyrazolidinium ylides containing a cyclic N–N bond of the dipolar form afford a variety of wide varieties of dinitrogen fused pyrazolopyrazolones, often key structural motifs within a plethora of various pharmaceuticals as well as agrochemicals.
headline: Fully Human Written
